name: Bodhidharma
description: Bodhidharma, also known as Daruma in Japan and Damo in China, is a semi-legendary Buddhist monk traditionally credited as the founder of Zen Buddhism and the transmitter of Chan Buddhism to China. While the historical details of his life are debated and often intertwined with legend, he is believed to have lived during the 5th or 6th century CE. Accounts vary, but most place his origins in South India, possibly near Kanchipuram, from where he traveled to China. He is said to have been a prince of the Pallava dynasty. Bodhidharma's teachings emphasized meditation and the direct experience of reality, rather than reliance on scriptures or rituals. He is renowned for his intense meditation practices, including the legendary nine years he spent facing a wall in silent contemplation at the Shaolin Monastery. This unwavering focus is a central aspect of his iconography. Bodhidharma is also associated with the physical training of the Shaolin monks, although the extent of his involvement in the development of Shaolin Kung Fu is a matter of ongoing debate. His influence extends beyond religious and martial practices; he is a cultural icon, a symbol of perseverance, enlightenment, and the unwavering pursuit of inner peace. Bodhidharma's legacy continues to inspire practitioners of Zen Buddhism and martial arts around the world, representing a bridge between Indian philosophical traditions and East Asian culture. He embodies the transformative power of disciplined meditation and the potential for direct realization of truth. His teachings and example remain a cornerstone of Zen practice and a source of fascination for scholars and spiritual seekers alike.
subtitle: Founder of Zen Buddhism, Wall-Facing Meditator
achievements: Practiced nine years of wall-facing meditation
Influenced the development of Shaolin Kung Fu

keyMyths.title: The Nine Years of Wall-Facing Meditation
keyMyths.description: Bodhidharma is said to have spent nine years meditating in front of a wall, demonstrating unwavering focus and determination. This act symbolizes the intense commitment required for enlightenment in Zen practice. It is said that his legs atrophied and fell off.

keyMyths.title: The Meeting with Emperor Wu of Liang
keyMyths.description: Bodhidharma's meeting with Emperor Wu of Liang is a famous anecdote where he declared that the Emperor's good deeds had no merit, highlighting the importance of emptiness and direct realization. This encounter emphasized the difference between conventional merit and true enlightenment. The emperor was not impressed.

keyMyths.title: The Transmission of Chan Buddhism
keyMyths.description: Bodhidharma is credited with transmitting Chan Buddhism to China, establishing a lineage of masters who emphasized meditation and direct experience. He is considered the First Patriarch of Chan, setting the stage for its development and spread throughout East Asia. He passed down the Lankavatara Sutra.

sources.author: D.T. Suzuki

sources.text: A foundational text on Zen Buddhism, providing insights into Bodhidharma's teachings and influence.

sources.author: John R. McRae
sources.work: The Northern School and the Formation of Early Ch'an Buddhism
sources.description: A scholarly work that examines the historical context and development of early Chan Buddhism, including the role of Bodhidharma.
